text:
Designing Pb-free relaxors that simultaneously possess high capacitive energy density ($W_{rec}$) and high storage efficiency ($\eta$) is a critical challenge for advancing pulsed power technologies. While local compositional heterogeneity is known to drive complex polar structures in solid solution relaxors, its potential for optimizing energy storage performance has remained largely untapped. Here, we report an exceptionally high $W_{rec}$ of 15.2 J cm⁻³ coupled with an ultrahigh $\eta$ of 91% in $Bi_{0.5}Na_{0.5}TiO_3$-$BaTiO_3$-based relaxors, achieved by specifically engineering local chemical clustering. 

Using a three-dimensional atomistic model constructed from neutron/X-ray total scattering and reverse Monte Carlo simulations, we identify sub-nanometer clusters of Bi, Na, and Ba. These clusters host distinct polar displacements, confirming predictions from density functional theory. This unique arrangement generates a polar state characterized by small clusters and significant fluctuations in both the magnitude and direction of unit-cell polar vectors. Macroscopically, this translates to enhanced high-field polarizability, minimal hysteresis, and superior breakdown strength. Furthermore, these structural features result in an unusual field-driven increase in efficiency, excellent cycling stability, and high discharge capacity. Our findings demonstrate that hidden local chemical order significantly dictates the polarization dynamics of relaxors and can be strategically utilized to reach new frontiers in energy storage performance.
